title: OpenAI ships gpt-realtime-2.1 with 25% lower voice latency and a cheaper mini tier
subtitle: The new Realtime API models cut p95 latency by at least a quarter, sharpen alphanumeric recognition and interruption handling, and add a mini variant priced to make voice agents economically viable for the first time.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: OpenAI released gpt-realtime-2.1 and gpt-realtime-2.1-mini in the Realtime API this week, replacing the previous GPT-Realtime-2 as the recommended path for low-latency voice and multimodal agents. The company reports at least a 25 percent reduction in p95 latency across the Realtime voice line, driven mostly by improved caching, and cites at least a 10 percent lower word error rate versus the prior production model in ChatGPT dictation evaluations across top languages. The full model adds better alphanumeric recognition, silence and noise handling, interruption behavior, and configurable reasoning effort with tool use. The mini tier is priced at $10 per million audio input tokens and $20 per million audio output — roughly a third of the full-model price of $32 in and $64 out.

The mini pricing is the most consequential piece. Voice agents have been stuck in a demo-that-doesn't-ship pattern for two years, and the reason has usually been unit economics: a five-minute customer call at older Realtime prices was not something a support desk could afford to run at scale. A one-third cost cut moves those calls into the range where a mid-market SaaS company can actually put an agent on the phone. The latency drop matters for a different reason — below a threshold, voice agents stop feeling like an IVR menu and start feeling like a conversation, and p95 is the number that decides whether the outlier calls still fall on the wrong side of that line.

The context here is that the voice-agent market has crystallized fast. ElevenLabs crossed $500 million in ARR earlier this year. Assort Health closed a $120 million Series C for patient-intake voice AI. Zoom is buying Common Room to attach agent-driven buyer intelligence to its meeting product. The stack for building voice agents — model, tool use, telephony, memory — has been assembling piece by piece, and OpenAI's release is the piece most builders were waiting on. Expect the next twelve months to look like the chatbot boom of 2023, only in the voice channel: a lot of the same builders shipping a lot of the same use cases, and a much smaller share of them making it to real production.

A takeaway for learners: if you are picking a project to build in the second half of 2026, voice agents on the Realtime API are unusually ripe. The tooling is now good enough that a solo builder can ship a working voice product in a weekend, and the pricing has finally dropped into the range where such a product could break even. The skills to develop are prompt design for turn-taking, evaluation of interruption and noise-handling behavior, and the boring-but-decisive work of measuring p95 latency in your actual deployment. The demo is easy. The production reliability is the moat.
